metadata.title: Generate a Reusable Workflow Card from a Successful Task

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: I love that the NEEDS HUMAN REVIEW flags are treated as valuable as the filled fields, and that merging two runs took the more specific stop conditions and the shorter prompt template. I want to run the full capture loop on my own recurring task. Walk me through (1) extracting all 15 fields from one completed brief and listing exactly which ones flag as gaps, (2) running a second instance and diffing the two cards field by field, and (3) writing the merge rule that resolves conflicts into one canonical card.

props.segment: Generate a Reusable Workflow Card from

beats.narration_text: The difference between Claude helped me once and Claude is a reliable part of this process is the capture step.

A completed task brief contains everything needed to define a reusable workflow — the trigger, the prompt template, the output format, the review checklist, the stop conditions. The 15-field workflow card format exists to extract this knowledge before it disappears into the task log.
From this completed task brief and output, extract a reusable workflow card. Fill all 15 fields. Where information is missing, flag as NEEDS HUMAN REVIEW.

props.command: claude "From this completed task brief and output, extract a reusable workflow card. Fill all 15 fields: Task Type, Trigger, Input Format, Prompt Template, Allowed Sources, Exclusions, Output Artifact, Review Checklist, Stop Conditions, Frequency, Owner, Quality Gate, Archive Destination, Version, Notes. Where information is missing, flag as [NEEDS HUMAN REVIEW]." < completed_task.md

beats.narration_text: The workflow card script reads the completed task, extracts all 15 fields, flags gaps as NEEDS HUMAN REVIEW, and writes the card to markdown.

props.code:
CARD_FIELDS = [
    "Task Type", "Trigger", "Input Format", "Prompt Template",
    "Allowed Sources", "Exclusions", "Output Artifact", "Review Checklist",
    "Stop Conditions", "Frequency", "Owner", "Quality Gate",
    "Archive Destination", "Version", "Notes"
]

def extract_card(task_brief: dict, task_output: dict) -> dict:
    card = {}
    for field in CARD_FIELDS:
        value = task_brief.get(field) or task_output.get(field)
        card[field] = value if value else "[NEEDS HUMAN REVIEW]"
    return card

def write_card(card: dict, output_path: str):
    lines = ["# Workflow Card\n"]
    for field, value in card.items():
        flag = " ⚠" if value == "[NEEDS HUMAN REVIEW]" else ""
        lines.append(f"## {field}{flag}\n{value}\n")
    with open(output_path, "w") as f:
        f.write("\n".join(lines))
    needs_review = sum(1 for v in card.values() if v == "[NEEDS HUMAN REVIEW]")
    print(f"Card written. {needs_review} fields need human review.")

beats.narration_text: 15-field card generated. 12 fields populated from the task brief and output. 3 fields flagged NEEDS HUMAN REVIEW: Frequency — not mentioned in the brief; Owner — implicit, needs explicit assignment; Archive Destination — no storage location specified. The flags are as valuable as the populated fields — they're the gaps that would have caused problems on the next run.
Run extraction on a second instance of the same task type; merge into a canonical workflow card.

Two instances differ on Prompt Template — different phrasing, same intent — and Stop Conditions — instance 2 has more specific criteria. Merge resolves: canonical card takes the more specific Stop Conditions and the shorter Prompt Template. The canonical card is now derived from two actual runs, not one — more reliable than either instance alone.

The 15-field workflow card transforms a one-time success into a repeatable process. The NEEDS HUMAN REVIEW flags are not failures — they're the fields where the human must add knowledge that did not exist in the task log. Fill them once and the card is complete.
Next: build the weekly operations packet — the capstone CoWork demonstration.
Nik Bear Brown. Brutalist and Educational AI. nikbearbrown.com.

beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.
